topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a dropped stone in a still pond. Professor Albright was departing. The subject line was curt: "Resignation." I reread it three times, my thumb hovering over the 'reply' button before letting my hand drop onto the desk. My pen, usually a steady extension of my will, felt suddenly heavy, the ink refusing to flow onto the page of my outline. I stared at the framed photograph on my monitor—Albright smiling, a gesture I hadn't seen in weeks. A strange, hollow sensation settled in my chest, a sort of quiet disorientation. I stood up, walked to the window, and watched the campus quad. Everyone seemed to be moving with a normal rhythm, oblivious to the seismic shift happening in the academic structure. I felt a peculiar tightness behind my eyes, a kind of static buzzing beneath my skin, as if my internal compass had momentarily spun off true north.

[story 2]

"So, he's actually leaving?" I managed to ask, the words sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. Dr. Vance leaned back in his leather chair, the sound of the springs protesting the movement. His expression was carefully neutral, the kind of placid mask he usually wore during intense departmental discussions. But today, even his composure seemed brittle. I watched the way his fingers drummed a silent rhythm on the mahogany table. My jaw felt suddenly slack. I tried to formulate a question about the implications for my dissertation timeline, but the air caught in my throat. It wasn't just the loss of a mentor; it was the sudden erasure of the known landscape of my research path. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, and found myself tracing the rim with my index finger, a repetitive, almost frantic motion. Everything felt slightly out of focus, like viewing the world through a pane of slightly warped glass.

[story 3]

I kept glancing at the door, half-expecting Professor Albright to materialize, perhaps with a hastily scribbled note. The silence in the office was oppressive, thick with unspoken finality. When the notification finally registered, it didn't register as information; it registered as a sudden drop in temperature. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I leaned forward, resting my elbows on the desk, my gaze fixed on the screen where the official announcement resided. My mind went blank, a disconcerting void where complex thought usually resided. I blinked several times, trying to force a coherent thought structure back into place, but all that surfaced was a shimmering uncertainty. I pushed my chair back abruptly, the scrape of the wheels echoing loudly in the quiet room. I needed to move, to do something tangible, but my limbs felt weighted, slow to respond, as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold.
